barcelona: The Sagrada Família is a giant basilica in Barcelona designed by Antoni Gaudí; it has been under construction for more than 140 years and its towers look like drip sandcastles.
Park Güell is a colorful park in Barcelona full of wavy benches covered in broken-tile mosaics called trencadís; a mosaic lizard named the dragon guards the entrance.
La Rambla is a long walking street in the middle of Barcelona where street performers freeze like statues and flower stalls line the path.
Barcelona sits on the Mediterranean Sea and Barceloneta beach is right next to the city, so kids can build sandcastles after sightseeing.
Casa Batlló is a Gaudí house with a roof like a dragon's back and balconies that look like carnival masks.
Paella is a famous Spanish rice dish cooked in a wide flat pan, often yellow from saffron and topped with shrimp and peas.
Churros are fried dough sticks that Spanish kids love to dip into thick hot chocolate, especially for breakfast.
Pan con tomate is a Barcelona favorite: toasted bread rubbed with ripe tomato, olive oil and a little salt.
In Barcelona many people speak both Spanish and Catalan; 'hello' is 'hola' and 'thank you' is 'gracias'.
The Magic Fountain of Montjuïc puts on evening shows where water dances to music and changes color.
FC Barcelona plays football at Camp Nou, one of the biggest stadiums in Europe.
Barcelona's old Gothic Quarter has narrow stone streets where you can find hidden squares and gelato shops.
tokyo: Cherry blossoms (sakura) bloom in Tokyo every spring and families have picnics under the pink trees, a tradition called hanami.
The Tokyo Skytree is one of the tallest towers in the world and looks like a giant silver needle reaching the clouds.
In Tokyo you can ride the Shinkansen bullet train, which zooms as fast as 320 km/h and always leaves exactly on time.
Sushi is rice with fish or vegetables, often wrapped in seaweed called nori; conveyor-belt sushi restaurants deliver plates on little trains.
Ramen is a hot noodle soup and slurping your noodles loudly in Japan is polite — it means the food is delicious.
At Shinto shrines in Tokyo, people ring a bell, clap twice and make a wish.
Koi are big friendly carp fish that swim in Japanese garden ponds; some live for more than 40 years.
'Hello' in Japanese is 'konnichiwa' and 'thank you' is 'arigatou'.
paris: The Eiffel Tower in Paris sparkles with twenty thousand lights for five minutes every evening hour after dark.
Croissants are flaky crescent-shaped pastries that Parisians eat for breakfast with hot chocolate.
The Louvre is the world's biggest art museum and its entrance is a giant glass pyramid.
A baguette is a long crunchy bread stick; French law even says what a real baguette must be made of.
Kids in Paris sail little wooden boats on the round pond in the Luxembourg Gardens.
'Hello' in French is 'bonjour' and 'thank you' is 'merci'.
generic: Airplanes fly above the clouds where the sky is always sunny, even when it rains below.
Every country has its own special foods, words and games — trying them is the best part of traveling.
Travelers say 'hello' and 'thank you' in the local language to make new friends.
